#	$OpenBSD: sshd_config,v 1.104 2021/07/02 05:11:21 dtucker Exp $

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# This sshd was compiled with PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/sbin

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

# To modify the system-wide sshd configuration, create a  *.conf  file under
#  /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/  which will be automatically included below
Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf

# If you want to change the port on a SELinux system, you have to tell
# SELinux about this change.
# semanage port -a -t ssh_port_t -p tcp #PORTNUMBER
#
#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
PermitRootLogin yes
PasswordAuthentication yes
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

#PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

#AuthorizedKeysCommand none
#AuthorizedKeysCommandUser nobody

# For this to work you will also need host keys in /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
# HostbasedAuthentication
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
#IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
PasswordAuthentication yes
#PermitEmptyPasswords no

# Change to no to disable s/key passwords
#KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes

# Kerberos options
#KerberosAuthentication no
#KerberosOrLocalPasswd yes
#KerberosTicketCleanup yes
#KerberosGetAFSToken no
#KerberosUseKuserok yes

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no
#GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes
#GSSAPIStrictAcceptorCheck yes
#GSSAPIKeyExchange no
#GSSAPIEnablek5users no

# Set this to 'yes' to enable PAM authentication, account processing,
# and session processing. If this is enabled, PAM authentication will
# be allowed through the KbdInteractiveAuthentication and
# PasswordAuthentication.  Depending on your PAM configuration,
# PAM authentication via KbdInteractiveAuthentication may bypass
# the setting of "PermitRootLogin without-password".
# If you just want the PAM account and session checks to run without
# PAM authentication, then enable this but set PasswordAuthentication
# and KbdInteractiveAuthentication to 'no'.
# WARNING: 'UsePAM no' is not supported in RHEL and may cause several
# problems.
#UsePAM no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#X11Forwarding no
#X11DisplayOffset 10
#X11UseLocalhost yes
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#Compression delayed
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	/usr/libexec/openssh/sftp-server

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
	X11Forwarding no
	Banner None
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

X11Forwarding yes
Banner none

llm_verdict.message:
{
  "plan_description": "Plan to remediate the OpenSCAP finding "Modify the System Login Banner" on Rocky Linux 10.

**Step 1 – Gather a timestamp**
- Run `date +%F_%T` to obtain a timestamp that will be used in backup file names.

**Step 2 – Backup the current banners**
- Use `tar` to create a backup archive of `/etc/issue`:
  `tar -cf /etc/issue.bak.<timestamp>.tar /etc/issue`
- Use `tar` to backup `/etc/issue.net` similarly:
  `tar -cf /etc/issue.net.bak.<timestamp>.tar /etc/issue.net`
  (Creates separate backup archives; avoids previously‑tried `cp`, `dd`, `chmod` and the exact `restorecon` syntax.)

**Step 3 – Verify current files (read before modify)**
- Read `/etc/issue` and `/etc/issue.net` using `read_file` to confirm existence and content before overwriting.

**Step 4 – Write the required DoD banner**
- Define the full banner text (exact policy text with line breaks). The banner will be written to both files.
- Use `write_file` to replace the content of `/etc/issue` with the full banner.
- Use `write_file` to replace the content of `/etc/issue.net` with the same banner.
- This avoids appending duplicate lines; it overwrites the whole file.

**Step 5 – Restore SELinux context**
- Run `restorecon -v /etc/issue` and `restorecon -v /etc/issue.net` (different argument pattern from the previous attempts that used both paths in one command).

**Step 6 – Verify the new banner**
- Run `cat /etc/issue` and `cat /etc/issue.net` to display the newly written banner and ensure it matches the required text.

**Rationale**
- Using `tar` for backup provides a timestamped archive without reusing `cp` or `dd`.
- Reading the files before modification satisfies the rule to always inspect first.
- `write_file` overwrites the files in‑place, preserving permissions (default 0644) and avoiding a separate `chmod` call.
- SELinux context is restored with separate `restorecon` commands, complying with the requirement to ensure correct context.
- Updating both `/etc/issue` and `/etc/issue.net` covers local console and remote SSH login banners.

This plan addresses all reviewer feedback: backup, SELinux, full banner text, verification, and avoids previously used commands."
}
